--- SOURCE 1: Trends in 2025 for Smart Cities ---
URL: https://www.capgemini.com/insights/expert-perspectives/trends-in-2025-for-smart-cities/

SUMMARY:
Smart cities are rising to the challenge, integrating advanced technologies to improve infrastructure, enhance public services, and foster sustainable living. Urban data platforms serve as the essential infrastructure for effectively utilizing city data to enhance operational efficiency and scale smart city initiatives. Digital twins and IoT technologies are shaping optimized city operations feeding off data from urban data platforms. Digital twins and citiverse initiatives are **redefining city operations** by making urban environments **more efficient, resilient, and citizen-friendly**. “We take cities and citizens on a journey to address societal challenges using digitization and data: Tackling environmental, sustainability and mobility issues, improving quality of life, overcoming societal divides, and supporting economic development.

Top Trends Shaping the Future of Smart Cities in 2025
=====================================================

by [Emilie Bramly](https://thinkz.ai/author/emilie/ "Posts by Emilie Bramly") | Jan 27, 2025 | [IoT](https://thinkz.ai/category/iot-connected-technology/), [Smart Cities](https://thinkz.ai/category/smart-cities-blog/)

![Image 15: Smart Cities Trends 2025 by Thinkz Libellium, Dinycon, Monotch, AI, IoT, Europe](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Top-Trends-Shaping-the-Future-of-Smart-Cities-in-2025-1080x675.png)

As we look toward 2025, the future of **smart cities** is being reshaped by cutting-edge technologies such as **AI** and **IoT**. **Smart city trends for 2025** will not only focus on the digitalisation of urban environments, but on creating **more efficient**, **sustainable**, and **responsive** cities that improve the quality of life for their citizens. Integrating**AI**and IoT will be pivotal in transforming how cities operate, optimising resources, and enhancing sustainability.

### **Liat Eyan, [VP R&D at Thinkz](https://www.linkedin.com/in/liat-eytan-77b026b6/), Predictions for 2025**

![Image 16](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/team_template.png)

“As we move towards 2025, I believe AI and IoT will be the driving forces behind transforming cities into smarter, more sustainable environments. Real-time data, validated through AI-powered solutions, will be pivotal in enabling cities to make faster, more accurate decisions. The integration of smart mobility systems and eco-friendly technologies will be essential to improving urban efficiency and reducing environmental impact. Moreover, the growing importance of data security and privacy will lead to more robust frameworks for handling urban data. At Thinkz, we are committed to supporting cities in harnessing these technologies to create a more connected and sustainable future.”

In this article, we explore the **major trends** that will define **smart cities in 2025**, offering a glimpse of what’s to come for urban spaces around the world.

**1st TREND – The Rise of AIoT: Transforming Cities Through Connected Intelligence**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 17: Smart Cities Trends 2025 - The Rise of AIoT Transforming Cities Through Connected Intelligence](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/The-Rise-of-AIoT-Transforming-Cities-Through-Connected-Intelligence-1024x590.png)
**AIoT** (Artificial Intelligence of Things) will be one of the most defining **smart cities trends in 2025**. With the rise of interconnected IoT devices, cities will generate vast amounts of data, and AI will process this data in real-time, enabling **more informed decision-making**. Traffic management systems will leverage AI to adjust traffic lights and optimise urban flow, while **air quality monitoring systems** will use AI to identify pollution levels and recommend actions to mitigate environmental risks.

For example, **traffic management systems** will use AI to process data from sensors and cameras, predicting traffic patterns and dynamically adjusting traffic light timings. Similarly, **air quality monitoring** systems will help cities respond to environmental concerns by detecting pollution levels and suggesting real-time actions to improve air quality.

**Key Benefit**: AIoT will enable cities to act on real-time data, making them more **responsive**, **efficient**, and **sustainable**.

**2nd TREND – The Rise of AIoT: Transforming Cities Through Connected Intelligence**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 18: Smart Cities Trends 2025- Predictive Analytics for Smarter Urban Management](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/2.-Predictive-Analytics-for-Smarter-Urban-Management-1024x590.png)
By 2025, **predictive analytics** powered by **AI** will be indispensable in urban planning. This technology will enable cities to forecast **traffic congestion**, **energy demand**, and environmental hazards, ensuring proactive measures can be taken to prevent disruptions. Cities like **London** are already using AI-based predictive models for better traffic flow management, adjusting transport services and policies based on predictive insights.

For example, cities like **[London](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024017419)** are already using predictive models to manage traffic congestion. By analysing real-time data, AI can forecast which areas will experience high congestion, allowing authorities to reroute traffic or adjust public transport schedules to minimise disruption.

**Key Benefit**: Predictive analytics allows cities to plan ahead, optimising resources and preventing issues before they arise.

****3rd TREND – Digital Twins: A Virtual Model for Smarter Planning****
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 19: Smart Cities Trends 2025 - Digital Twins A Virtual Model for Smarter Planning](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/3.-Digital-Twins-A-Virtual-Model-for-Smarter-Planning-1024x590.png)
**Digital Twins**—virtual replicas of physical urban environments—will become essential tools for **urban management** in 2025. By integrating **real-time data** from **IoT sensors**, **Digital Twins** allow city planners to simulate and analyse different scenarios before implementing physical changes. Cities like **Aachen** in Germany are already exploring **Digital Twin** technology to improve traffic flow and integrate renewable energy systems.

Cities like [**Aachen** in Germany](https://urban-mobility-observatory.transport.ec.europa.eu/resources/case-studies/digital-twins-lessons-learned-city-aachen_en#:~:text=The%20City%20of%20Aachen%20is,new%20standard%20for%20smart%20cities.) are already implementing **Digital Twin** technology. By creating a virtual model of the city, officials can test different urban development scenarios, such as changes to traffic flow or the introduction of renewable energy sources, before making any physical changes.

**Key Benefit**: **Digital Twins** enable smarter planning, simulating urban scenarios for better decision-making and optimising resource allocation.

******4th TREND – Sustainable Solutions: A Priority for Eco-Friendly Cities******
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 20: Smart Cities Trends 2025 - Sustainable Solutions A Priority for Eco-Friendly Cities](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Sustainable-Solutions-A-Priority-for-Eco-Friendly-Cities-1024x590.png)
Sustainability will remain a top priority for cities in 2025. IoT-enabled solutions will help cities manage energy, water, and waste more efficiently. **Smart grids** that integrate renewable energy sources and manage energy distribution will become the norm. For example, **Amsterdam** already uses smart grids to monitor energy consumption, balancing demand and optimising resource usage across the city.

For example, [**Amsterdam** uses smart grids](https://www.iamsterdam.com/en/business/how-smart-grids-are-enabling-amsterdams-energy-transition)to monitor and optimise energy use throughout the city. By integrating renewable energy sources such as wind and solar, **IoT-enabled smart grids** help ensure a steady, sustainable energy supply. Similarly, **Thinkz**‘s real-time data solutions help cities optimise energy usage, manage pollution levels, and reduce waste.

**Key Benefit**: Sustainable technologies will reduce the environmental footprint of cities, making them more **efficient** and **eco-friendly**.

********5th TREND – Intelligent Waste Management: Making Cities Cleaner and More Efficient********
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 21: Smart Cities Trends 2025 - Intelligent Waste Management Making Cities Cleaner and More Efficient](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Intelligent-Waste-Management-Making-Cities-Cleaner-and-More-Efficient-1024x590.png)
The future of waste management in smart cities will rely on IoT sensors to monitor waste levels and optimise collection routes. Smart waste systems like those already deployed in **Paris** will provide real-time data to reduce unnecessary truck trips, cut down on fuel consumption, and lower emissions, making cities cleaner and more efficient.

For example, **Paris** has deployed **smart bins** that use sensors to track fill levels. The data from these sensors is used to optimise the collection schedule, ensuring that bins are emptied at the right time, reducing truck traffic and lowering emissions.

[See the example of Thinkz UseCase Nicosia](https://thinkz.ai/smart-city-use-cases/#nicosia)

**Key Benefit**: **Intelligent waste management** systems can save cities money, reduce pollution, and make urban environments cleaner and more efficient.

**6th TREND – AI-Driven Disaster Prevention and Response**
----------------------------------------------------------

![Image 22: Smart Cities Trends 2025: AI-Driven Disaster Prevention and Response valencia](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/AI-Driven-Disaster-Prevention-and-Response-1024x590.png)
Disaster prevention and response will see a significant transformation in 2025, as **AI** and **IoT** technologies enable cities to better predict and mitigate the effects of natural disasters. By integrating real-time data from sensors placed in key locations, AI systems will be able to predict disasters like **floods**, **earthquakes**, or **wildfires** and provide early warnings to help reduce damage.

**Example**: In **Valencia**, Spain, IoT sensors monitor rainfall and river levels, and AI models use this data to predict flood events. The system provides early warnings that allow city officials to deploy flood barriers and evacuate areas at risk. As climate change increases the frequency and severity of such events, **AI-driven disaster response** will become essential to protecting urban populations.

**Key Benefit**: **AI-driven disaster prevention** will enable cities to take immediate action, reducing damage and saving lives in the event of natural disasters.

**7th TREND – Smart Mobility and Transport Management**
-------------------------------------------------------

![Image 23: Smart Cities Trends 2025: Smart Mobility and Transport Management](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Smart-Mobility-and-Transport-Management-1024x590.png)
The future of urban mobility will be **redefined by smart technologies** in 2025. Cities will implement AI-powered traffic management systems, **smart public transport** that adapts to real-time data, and **connected vehicles** to reduce congestion and make commuting more efficient.

In **Copenhagen**, smart traffic systems use **real-time data** to adjust traffic light timings based on current traffic conditions. Thinkz’s solutions for **real-time traffic management** could help cities like Copenhagen further optimise traffic flow and reduce congestion, enhancing urban mobility for both citizens and visitors.

**Key Benefit**: **Smart mobility** will streamline urban transport, making it more sustainable, efficient, and responsive to the needs of residents.

**8th TREND – Smart Grids and Energy Efficiency**
-------------------------------------------------

![Image 24: Smart Cities Trends 2025: Smart Grids and Energy Efficiency](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Smart-Grids-and-Energy-Efficiency-1024x590.png)
Smart grids will continue to evolve, helping cities manage energy distribution more effectively. These IoT-enabled systems monitor energy consumption in real-time, adjusting energy flow based on demand and incorporating renewable energy sources.

For example, **California** uses a **smart grid system** to manage energy consumption. The grid adjusts energy flow based on demand, reducing consumption during peak hours and ensuring that renewable energy is utilised efficiently.

**Key Benefit**: **Smart grids** will improve the efficiency of energy distribution, reduce carbon emissions, and support the integration of renewable energy sources.

****9th TREND – AI-Powered Citizen and Tourist Engagement****
-------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 25: Smart Cities Trends 2025: AI-Powered Citizen and Tourist Engagement](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/AI-Powered-Citizen-and-Tourist-Engagement-1024x590.png)

This system enhances both the **citizen** and **tourist experience** by offering intuitive, accessible information and helping to reduce congestion and improve mobility.

**BONUS by [Libelium](https://www.libelium.com/): IoT, Digital Twins, AI Services, and Data Spaces in Smart Cities Trends for 2025**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 27: libelium logo Thinkz partner IoT](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Libelium-1024x297.png)

_“In 2025, IoT will be a cornerstone of smart cities, powering data-driven decision-making across various urban sectors. Libelium envisions a future where interconnected sensors, Digital Twins, and AI services work in synergy to provide a holistic understanding of city dynamics. These technologies will enable comprehensive environmental monitoring, from tracking air quality to managing waste more effectively, helping cities achieve ambitious sustainability targets and boost operational efficiency._

_The expansion of smart city infrastructure will see IoT sensors integrated with Digital Twins, creating virtual replicas of urban environments. These Digital Twins will allow cities to simulate, predict, and optimise processes such as traffic management, parking solutions, and energy distribution. Combined with AI services, this will enable real-time analysis of complex datasets, empowering urban centres to make proactive, informed decisions._

_Data spaces will play a pivotal role in fostering collaboration across stakeholders, ensuring seamless and secure data exchange between public and private entities. By leveraging open standards and interoperable platforms, cities can unlock the full potential of IoT and AI technologies to enhance public services and improve the quality of life for citizens and visitors alike._

_Libelium’s innovative IoT solutions, coupled with its commitment to enabling Digital Twins and data spaces, will continue to drive the evolution of smarter, more sustainable, and efficient urban environments in 2025 and beyond.”_

**BONUS by [Monotch](https://monotch.com/) – Smart Mobility Trend for 2025: Real-Time, Data-Driven Traffic Optimisation**
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 28: monotch smart mobility](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/monotch-1024x297.png)

_In 2025, the future of smart mobility will be defined by real-time, data-driven traffic management. With the increasing integration of AI and IoT, cities will rely on platforms like Traffic Live Exchange (TLEX®) to optimise urban mobility. Monotch’s TLEX® platform will lead this transformation, enabling cities to manage traffic flow, enhance safety, and provide live updates to road users in real-time by connecting all different stakeholders in this platform_

_As mobility becomes more connected, shared transport solutions such as e-scooters, bikes, and electric vehicles will seamlessly integrate into traffic systems. TLEX® will make this possible, breaking down data silos between different transportation modes, and facilitating AI-powered solutions to ensure smoother, more sustainable urban travel._

_By 2025, real-time data from TLEX® will empower cities to make faster, smarter decisions, reduce congestion, improve traffic efficiency, and contribute to greener, more sustainable urban environments._“

**BONUS by [Dinycon](https://www.dinycon.com/en/home): Urban Management Trends for 2025**
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 29: Dinycon logo Thinkz partner IoT](https://thinkz.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Dinycon-1024x297.png)

“_Urban management will evolve with a focus on AI-powered analytics to streamline city operations and decision-making processes. One example is Dinycon´s innovative implementation of virtual sensors, which unlike traditional whole deployments of physical sensors, leverage historical and real-time data, integrated through advanced AI algorithms, to generate accurate models of urban dynamics. This approach reduces costs by 35–45%, enhances data coverage through the generation of unlimited count points, and delivers comprehensive heat maps for better decision-making._

_High-quality data from IoT deployments, powered by advanced AI, will play a pivotal role in enabling preventive maintenance for urban management systems. By ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of sensor data, Dinycon´s AI can identify anomalies, detect patterns, and predict potential failures in city infrastructure before they occur. This proactive approach minimizes downtime, reduces maintenance costs, and extends the lifespan of assets. Enhanced data quality also allows for precise forecasting and resource allocation, ensuring city operations remain efficient and resilient while improving public services and infrastructure reliability._

_We trust AI-driven analytics and data quality algorithms will enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and improve city livability with smarter, more sustainable, and resilient urban environments for 2025 and beyond._“

**Conclusion: The Future of Smart Cities in 2025**
--------------------------------------------------

The next few years will see **AI** and **IoT** continue to revolutionise urban living. From **predictive analytics** and **smart mobility** to **intelligent waste management** and **eco-friendly urban solutions**, these technologies will enable cities to become more sustainable, efficient, and resilient. By 2025, smart cities will be powered by real-time data, AI-driven insights, and integrated IoT systems, creating environments where innovation enhances the quality of life for everyone.

**Thinkz’s real-time, AI-powered IoT solutions** are designed to help cities embrace these trends, offering insights that drive urban innovation and improve the quality of life. Let’s work together to create smarter, more sustainable cities of the future.

Abstract
--------

Sustainable development in smart cities involves the integration of advanced technologies and efficient urban management to address challenges such as population growth and resource optimization. These cities leverage information technologies to improve quality of life and promote more sustainable urban governance. Although scientific production in this field has grown significantly, important gaps remain that require in-depth analysis. This study aims to examine research trends on the topic through a bibliometric analysis of 427 publications retrieved from Scopus and Web of Science, following the PRISMA 2020 guidelines. The results reveal growing interest, with significant publication peaks in 2021 and 2023. China (28.1%), the United States (16.6%), and India (9.4%) emerge as the most productive countries. Among the most influential institutions are Tongji University and Tsinghua University, while _Sustainability_ leads as the most prolific journal (18.5%). These findings reflect Asia’s leadership in generating knowledge in this area. Thematic evolution reveals a shift toward emerging topics such as the Internet of Things (IoT), artificial intelligence, and resilience, forming key conceptual clusters. The emergence of keywords such as “energy efficiency” and “smart mobility” points to promising areas for future research. Key authors like Bibri, Yigitcanlar, and Krogstie provide solid theoretical frameworks to guide upcoming studies. Overall, the findings underscore the need for global collaboration to address sustainability challenges in increasingly urbanized and technologically driven societies.

1 Introduction
--------------

Sustainable development officially introduced in 1987 through the Brundtland Report, developed by the World Commission on Environment and Development. In this document, it is described as a development model that seeks to meet present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own. Since then, the concept has evolved into a broader vision that integrates environmental, social, and economic aspects, emphasizing intergenerational justice, natural resource conservation, and energy efficiency [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR1 "Faustino A, Mantovaneli Junior O, Barbosa VC. Concepção de desenvolvimento sustentável para a governança internacional Análise dos eventos históricos até a Agenda 2030. HALAC Hist Ambiental Latinoam y Caribeña. 2024;14(3):385–419. 
                  https://doi.org/10.32991/2237-2717.2024v14i3.p385-419
                  
                .")].

This approach has gained relevance in an urbanized world, where urban centers are responsible for significant resource consumption and high carbon emissions. Megacities have become key players in global change, standing out for their high consumption of resources and energy, as well as their substantial generation of waste and greenhouse gas emissions. These urban dynamics represent a major challenge for sustainability, as their proper management will be crucial to advancing toward resilient and sustainable human settlements in the future [[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR2 "Mackay BR, Shaker RR. A megacities review: comparing indicator-based evaluations of sustainable development and urban resilience. Sustainability. 2024;16(18):8076. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su16188076
                  
                .")].

On the other hand, since the early decades of the twenty-first century, the concept of the smart city has emerged as a strategy to address the challenges of urban growth, proposing the use of information and communication technologies (ICT) along with participatory governance models, digital infrastructure, and technological solutions aimed at improving quality of life, promoting sustainability, and optimizing resource use [[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR3 "Mozūriūnaitė S, Sabaitytė J. \"To what extent we do understand smart cities and characteristics influencing city smartness. J Architect Urban. 2021;45(1):1–8. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3846/jau.2021.12392
                  
                .")].

The notion of sustainable development in smart cities constitutes a key research domain at the intersection of advanced technology and urban management. In this paradigm, the objective is to address the predominant challenges faced by cities, such as population growth, operational efficiency, and mitigation of environmental impact. Smart cities are characterized by the integration of ICT to improve citizens' quality of life and optimize the use of urban resources [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR4 "Góngora GPM. Revisión de literatura sobre ciudades inteligentes: una perspectiva centrada en las TIC”. Ingeniare. 2015. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1804/1909-2458/ingeniare.19.531
                  
                .")].

Today, cities face complex challenges associated with population growth, rapid urbanization, and pressure on natural resources. In this context, the convergence of two key paradigms—smart cities and sustainable development—has given rise to a new area of research that aims to generate innovative solutions to contemporary problems such as urban mobility, energy management, logistical efficiency, environmental resilience, and citizen participation. This intersection relies on the use of emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, the Internet of Things (IoT), smart energy systems, and real-time data analysis, shaping a dynamic scenario of urban transformation.

In recent years, a substantial body of studies has emerged addressing various facets of this phenomenon.

For example, Ibrahim et al. [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR5 "Ibrahim HA, et al. Sustainability in mobility for autonomous vehicles over smart city evaluation; using interval-valued fermatean fuzzy rough set-based decision-making model. Eng Appl Artif Intell. 2024;129: 107609. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.engappai.2023.107609
                  
                .")] developed a decision-making model based on interval-valued rough Fermatean fuzzy sets to evaluate the sustainability of autonomous vehicle mobility. Hashim et al. [[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR6 "Hashim N, Neo TG, Mohammed MN, Sultan HS, Jameel Al-Tamimi AN, Alfiras M. Toward sustainable smart cities: a new approach of solar and wind renewable energy in agriculture applications. In: Artificial intelligence and transforming digital marketing. Springer: Cham; 2023.")], in turn, proposed an approach focused on the application of renewable energies—solar and wind—in agricultural contexts, with direct implications for sustainable smart cities. Complementarily, Balfaqih et al. [[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR7 "Balfaqih M, Balfagih Z, Lytras MD, Alfawaz KM, Alshdadi AA, Alsolami E. A blockchain-enabled IoT logistics system for efficient tracking and management of high-price shipments: a resilient, scalable and sustainable approach to smart cities. Sustainability. 2023;15(18):13971. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su151813971
                  
                .")] explored the integration of blockchain and IoT for the efficient management of high-value shipments, highlighting their contribution to urban resilience. In line with these advances, Islam et al. [[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR8 "Islam S, Budati AK, Mohammad KH, Goyal SB, Raju D. A multi-sensory real-time data transmission method with sustainable and robust 5G energy signals for smart cities. Sustain Energy Technol Assess. 2023;57: 103278. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seta.2023.103278
                  
                .")] present a real-time data transmission method via 5G energy signals, contributing robustness and sustainability to the urban tech ecosystem. Alsaid and Mutiganda [[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR9 "Alsaid LAZA, Mutiganda JC. ABC as a multi-lens sustainability reporting system in smart cities. Sustainability. 2023;15(12):9357. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129357
                  
                .")] also propose the use of the ABC system as a sustainability reporting tool from a multidimensional perspective.

According to the thematic analysis by [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR10 "Nedungadi P, Surendran S, Tang K-Y, Raman R. Big data and AI algorithms for sustainable development goals: a topic modeling analysis. IEEE Access. 2024;12:188519–37. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3516500
                  
                .")], the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) most impacted by AI and Big Data are SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being), SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), and SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure).

These findings are particularly relevant in urban contexts, where smart cities can integrate solutions such as AI-assisted medical diagnosis, smart energy management, and predictive maintenance in urban infrastructures. Meanwhile, among the most prominent emerging topics related to SDG 12 are industrial symbiosis, the circular economy, e-waste management, material flow analysis, and communities' environmental attitudes. These topics not only reflect environmental concerns but also highlight critical challenges in contemporary urban management [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR11 "Raman R, Lathabai HH, Nedungadi P. Sustainable development goal 12 and its synergies with other SDGs: Identification of key research contributions and policy insights. Discov Sustain. 2024. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s43621-024-00289-0
                  
                .")].

These contributions reflect growing attention to sustainability as a central axis in the development of smart cities. The importance of this approach lies in its ability to respond to the challenges of the contemporary context, where the impact of cities on resource consumption, carbon emissions, and energy efficiency calls for new forms of governance and planning. Naranjo et al. [[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR12 "Naranjo FV, Vivar SM, Arias EJ, Atassi R. Early energy consumption prediction as a key element in smart city sustainability. J Intell Syst Int Things. 2023;11(1):12. 
                  https://doi.org/10.54216/JISIoT.110102
                  
                .")], for example, highlight the relevance of early energy consumption prediction as a key factor in reducing the urban environmental footprint, while Mohammed et al. [[13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR13 "Mohammed MN, Al-yousif S, Alfiras M, Rahman M, Al-Tamimi ANJ, Sharif A. Toward sustainable smart cities: design and development of piezoelectric-based footstep power generation system. In: Artificial intelligence and transforming digital marketing. Springer Nature Switzerland: Cham; 2023.")] explore alternative energy generation solutions through piezoelectric systems that harness pedestrian movement in public spaces.

Likewise, the implementation of innovative business models emerges as a key strategy to foster sustainable practices. Anthony Jr. [[14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR14 "Anthony B Jr. Data enabling digital ecosystem for sustainable shared electric mobility-as-a-service in smart cities-an innovative business model perspective. Res Transp Bus Manag. 2023;51: 101043. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rtbm.2023.101043
                  
                .")] analyzes a model based on shared electric mobility, where data management plays a key role in creating sustainable digital ecosystems. Meanwhile, Khurana and Dutta [[15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR15 "Khurana I, Dutta DK. From place to space: the emergence and evolution of sustainable entrepreneurial ecosystems in smart cities. Small Bus Econ. 2023. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11187-023-00765-3
                  
                .")] emphasize the emergence of sustainable entrepreneurial ecosystems in smart cities, highlighting the interconnection between urban sustainability and economic development.

Despite this growing body of knowledge, significant gaps still deserve further attention. Bibliometrics emerges as a key methodological tool to identify patterns, gaps, and opportunities in this field. In this regard, Santibáñez González et al. [[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR16 "Santibanez Gonzalez ED, Kandpal V, Machado M, Martens ML, Majumdar S. A Bibliometric analysis of circular economies through sustainable smart cities. Sustainability. 2023;15(22):15892. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su152215892
                  
                .")] offer an analysis focused on circular economies, although with limitations due to the exclusion of other relevant perspectives and methodologies. Similarly, Zaidi et al. [[17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR17 "Zaidi A, Ajibade SSM, Musa M, Bekun FV. New insights into the research landscape on the application of artificial intelligence in sustainable smart cities: a bibliometric mapping and network analysis approach. Int J Energy Econ Policy. 2023;13(4):287–99. 
                  https://doi.org/10.32479/ijeep.14683
                  
                .")] conducts a comprehensive review on the application of artificial intelligence in sustainable smart cities, although their study does not deeply address intersections with other dimensions of urban development.

Nevertheless, despite the increasing volume of studies published on this topic, relevant gaps remain in the literature. Various reviews have addressed aspects—such as electric mobility, renewable energy, or digital governance—but there is a lack of a systematic vision to identify trends, thematic patterns, theoretical frameworks, and research gaps from a bibliometric perspective. This study stands out by undertaking a bibliometric analysis with an emphasis on the thematic evolution of sustainable development in smart cities.

This research employs a systematic approach based on PRISMA-2020 and bibliometric mapping tools to identify emerging trends, thematic clusters, and gaps in the literature. Several studies have adopted systematic review protocols such as PRISMA to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and methodological rigor. According to [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR18 "Raman R, Lathabhai H, Pattnaik D, Kumar C, Nedungadi P. Research contribution of bibliometric studies related to sustainable development goals and sustainability. Discov Sustain. 2024. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s43621-024-00182-w
                  
                .")], the PRISMA protocol is the most widely used in systematic reviews related to the SDGs, being adopted in at least 156 analyzed articles, demonstrating its acceptance and relevance in bibliometric studies within the field of sustainability. In this scenario, the present research aims to explore trends in scientific production related to sustainable development in smart cities. To that end, the following research questions are posed:

Which years have shown the greatest interest in sustainable development in smart cities?

What is the growth rate of scientific articles on this topic?

What are the main research references on sustainable development in smart cities?

What is the thematic evolution derived from scientific production in this area?

What are the main thematic clusters of sustainable development in smart cities?

What are the emerging keywords in research on this topic?

Which topics emerge as key for designing a research agenda on sustainable development in smart cities?

The study is organized into six sections: an abstract, an introduction, a methodology section, a results section, a discussion section, and a conclusion. The abstract provides a concise overview of the study’s objectives, methods, findings, and conclusions. The introduction sets the context, justifies the research, and identifies the research questions of interest. The methodology describes the approaches and techniques used to collect and analyze data. The findings are presented objectively and descriptively. The discussion section interprets the results, connects them with existing literature, and suggests implications and future directions. Finally, the conclusion summarizes the key points and highlights the study's contribution to knowledge in this field, providing a clear response to the research questions posed.

2 Methodology
-------------

This study uses an exploratory methodology based on secondary sources, following the PRISMA-2020 protocol to conduct a systematic review on sustainable development in smart cities. The aim is to carry out a rigorous bibliometric analysis that identifies, evaluates, and synthesizes the existing scientific evidence, ensuring integrity, objectivity, systematicity, transparency, and reproducibility. Tools such as VOSviewer® are employed to visualize keyword co-occurrence networks and detect key thematic patterns [[19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR19 "Van Eck NJ, Waltman L, Dekker R, Van den Berg J. A comparison of two techniques for bibliometric mapping: multidimensional scaling and VOS. J Am Soc Inform Sci Technol. 2010;61(12):2405–16."), [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR20 "Van Eck NJ, Waltman L. Software survey: VOSviewer, a computer program for bibliometric mapping. Scientometrics. 2010;84(2):523–38.")].

Although previous studies such as [[21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR21 "Donthu N, Kumar S, Mukherjee D, Pandey N, Lim WM. How to conduct a bibliometric analysis: an overview and guidelines. J Bus Res. 2021;133:285–96.")] and [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR22 "Mukherjee D, Lim WM, Kumar S, Donthu N. Guidelines for advancing theory and practice through bibliometric research. J Bus Res. 2022;148:101–15.")] suggest incorporating advanced metrics in bibliometric analyses (such as thematic heterogeneity indices or normalized impact metrics), this work adopts a more structured and simplified approach. It is proposed that future research deepens the analysis using more sophisticated metrics to explore collaborative dynamics in sustainable smart cities.

The methodological choice to use bibliometric analysis focused on keyword co-occurrence aligns with previous studies that have applied similar strategies to explore thematic evolution in various scientific fields. For instance, research by [[23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR23 "Zyoud S. Mapping and visualizing global knowledge on intermittent water supply systems. Water. 2022;14(5):738.")] y [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-025-01210-z#ref-CR24 "Zyoud SH, Zyoud AH. Visualization and mapping of knowledge and science landscapes in Expert Systems with Applications journal: A 30 years’ bibliometric analysis. SAGE Open. 2021;11(2):21582440211027576.")] has demonstrated the effectiveness of analyzing metadata from established databases to map trends and conceptual relationships in sustainability-related areas.

### 2.1 Information sources and search strategy

The databases Scopus and Web of Science, recognized for their broad scientific coverage, were used as the primary sources. Specific search equations were designed for each database using key terms such as smart cities, sustainable development, and their synonyms (e.g., intelligent cities, automation cities). Operators such as sustainab** were also used to include relevant variants:

• Scopus: (TITLE ({Smart city} OR {Smart cities} OR {Intelligent city} OR {Intelligent cities} OR {Automation city} OR {Automation cities}) AND TITLE (sustainab* OR ecolog* OR ecodevelopment)).

• Web of Science: (TI = ({Smart city} OR {Smart cities} OR {Intelligent city} OR {Intelligent cities} OR {Automation city} OR {Automation cities}) AND TI = (sustainab* OR ecolog* OR ecodevelopment)).

The choice of Scopus and Web of Science as main sources for collecting bibliographic data is based on their broad interdisciplinary coverage, high editorial curation, and the standardized quality of the metadata they offer—essential aspects for ensuring reliability and replicability in bibliometric studies. Both platforms are widely recognized by the international scientific community as benchmarks for evaluating academic output.

Although other databases such as IEEE Xplore were considered, it was excluded due to its focus primarily on engineering and technology, which could bias the disciplinary diversity of the analysis. Similarly, Google Scholar was excluded due to the lack of metadata standardization and the impossibility of precisely replicating searches, which would compromise the methodological validity of the study.

### 2.2 Eligibility criteria and selection process

Document inclusion focused on titles and keywords combining terms related to smart cities and sustainability. To ensure the quality of the collected material, three exclusion phases were applied: first, records with incorrect indexing were removed; then, systematic reviews and documents without full text available were excluded; finally, conference proceedings and documents with incomplete indexing were discarded. Technologies such as digital twins and AI-driven analytics enable real-time decision-making, optimizing resource management and improving urban mobility.
